title: Boop Agent Tour
description: A guided walkthrough of boop-agent — the dispatcher/executor agent system behind iMessage, with Convex as source of truth.
prerequisites: Optional: skim the architecture doc for the four-part overview

steps.title: Welcome to boop-agent
steps.description: Boop is a small distributed system disguised as a single-server app — a web server in front, Convex for state, a debug dashboard for replay, and a handful of supporting scripts. This tour walks through each piece. Start here with the full codebase; the regions you see roughly map to the four main jobs.

steps.title: The runtime: server/
steps.description: Everything the live agent does happens in the server. The web entry boots an HTTP and WebSocket server, the webhook handler accepts incoming messages from the SMS provider, and a broadcast layer fans events out to the debug dashboard. Read this area top-down to understand a request's life.

steps.title: The dispatcher pattern: two agents
steps.description: Boop has two agents. The interaction agent is the front door — one per user turn, deciding what to do. The execution agent is spawned per task, ephemeral, runs the work and returns a string. This split is the load-bearing idea of the whole system.

interactiveActions.description: Open the dispatcher to see its system prompt and tool list

steps.title: Memory: extract, recall, decay
steps.description: The memory subsystem is four files doing four jobs: shapes and tiers, the memory tool server, post-turn fact extraction (fire-and-forget), and a decay loop that runs every six hours. Consolidation lives one level up.

steps.title: Automations: cron-driven sub-agents
steps.description: A thirty-second poll loop wakes up enabled cron entries. When one is due, it spawns an execution agent — the same machinery the dispatcher uses. A small tool surface lets the interaction agent create, list, toggle, and delete automations from any conversation.

steps.title: Integrations via Composio
steps.description: Boop delegates all third-party tools to Composio. A toolkit-scoped tool server is built fresh per spawn, so a sub-agent only ever sees the tools for the toolkits its task needs. A small set of HTTP routes handles authentication, disconnects, and registry refreshes.

steps.title: Convex: the source of truth
steps.description: All persistent state lives here. The schema defines roughly eleven tables — messages, memory records, execution agents, automations, drafts, and so on. Each sibling module is a set of queries and mutations consumed by the server and the debug dashboard. Read the schema first.

steps.title: The debug dashboard
steps.description: The debug dashboard is a read-mostly React app that subscribes to Convex. It's how you watch agents run, browse memories, manage drafts, and connect Composio toolkits. Treat it as a passive viewer — the server still owns all writes that matter.

steps.title: Scripts, docs, and configuration
steps.description: The scripts directory holds the dev runner, preflight checks, and SMS-sync helpers. The architecture doc is the canonical map; the integrations doc covers Composio specifics; the agent-author guides explain how Claude is expected to work inside the project. The example environment file is the contract for required keys.

steps.title: Where to go next
steps.description: You've seen the four parts: the server runtime, Convex state, the debug dashboard, and the surrounding scripts and docs. To dig deeper, read the architecture doc end-to-end, then trace a real request from the SMS webhook through the interaction agent into a spawned execution agent and watch it replay in the dashboard.
